picks.headline: 67-acre Ozark spring and creek homestead
picks.reason: 67.5 acres at $4,133/acre with an existing well, septic, spring, and creek eliminates the two most expensive infrastructure hurdles. Mixed timber and open ground in the Tomahawk Creek watershed support both resource harvesting and cultivation. The truncated listing leaves road access and electricity unconfirmed — those must be verified before closing, as they are the only material unknowns on an otherwise strong buy.

picks.headline: 53 acres with creek, pond, and springs
picks.reason: 53 acres at $4,000/acre in Madison County, AR delivers creek frontage, a pond, and springs — three independent water sources that underpin genuine off-grid self-sufficiency. Paved access and a build-ready classification reduce startup friction. Soil quality and whether a residential permit is straightforward in this county are the critical unknowns requiring due diligence before purchase.

picks.headline: 40-acre Ozark waterfall retreat with cabins
picks.reason: Year-round creeks and a waterfall in the Tomahawk Creek–Little Red River watershed provide reliable water on Class 3 (well-drained, 5% slope) Linker-Mountainburg soil suitable for small-scale agriculture. Existing cabins and on-site utilities enable immediate occupancy without a development phase. At $9,913/acre the price is the main trade-off — this is comfort homesteading, not a pioneer land play, and grid infrastructure means true off-grid operation would require retrofit.

picks.headline: 172 acres Class 2 soil, pioneer infrastructure build
picks.reason: 172 acres at $1,512/acre with Ruston fine sandy loam (USDA Class 2, 2% slope, excellent drainage) is the best soil-per-dollar combination in this set. No flood risk and solid agricultural ground give this genuine long-term homesteading upside. The catch is real: no well, no septic, no utilities, and the listing emphasizes hunting use — zoning for residential homestead must be confirmed before treating this as a buildable property, and infrastructure costs could run $50–100k.

picks.headline: 61 acres with four ponds near Highway 21
picks.reason: 61 acres at $6,250/acre in Madison County, AR comes with four ponds providing redundant water storage and livestock infrastructure without needing a drilled well. Less than 1 mile from Highway 21 and utilities available at the county road reduce both isolation and development cost. Soil quality is undisclosed and must be verified; at this price per acre the budget for soil amendment or raised-bed systems is limited.

picks.headline: 40-acre farm with house, workshop, and pond
picks.reason: 40.8 acres at $7,353/acre in Howell County, MO includes a farmhouse, workshop, fenced pasture, and pond — a functional livestock operation on day one. The 2-mile proximity to Willow Springs provides healthcare, feed, and hardware access without compromising rural character. Soil data is absent and the farmhouse age and condition are unverified; a structural inspection and soil sample should be conditions of any offer.

picks.headline: 30-acre farmstead with barns and spring pond
picks.reason: Existing farmhouse, barns, and agricultural infrastructure with a spring-fed pond 10 minutes from town make this the most immediately livable option in the set. At $13,967/acre for 30 developed acres in Texas County, MO it is priced for the improvements, not raw land. The main risk is that spring reliability and soil suitability are unverified — a dry year or soil test revealing poor ground could undermine the agricultural case at this price.

picks.headline: 8.78-acre spring parcel with Class 3 soil
picks.reason: A natural spring combined with Weller silt loam (Class 3, farmland of statewide importance, 6.2% slope, flood zone X) is an unusually strong combination at this price point in Missouri. Off-grid viability is tagged and the terrain is buildable with minimal earthwork. The acreage is tight at 8.78 acres — barely enough for a full homestead without intensive management — and road access and utility hookup must be confirmed with the seller before closing.

picks.headline: 21-acre build-ready parcel with fiber and water
picks.reason: 21.8 acres at $11,927/acre in Newton County (Ozarks, AR) with water, electric, and fiber internet already on site eliminates the three most common rural development delays. Highway access on both sides simplifies easement and logistics. Soil data and water rights detail are absent, which matters at this price — agricultural potential cannot be confirmed without a site visit and soil test, so this suits a homesteader who prioritizes connectivity and infrastructure over farming.

picks.headline: 6-acre stream parcel with agricultural soil
picks.reason: 6.09 acres with a year-round stream and Class 3 Weller silt loam (6.2% slope, flood zone X) at $7,537/acre provides quality agricultural ground and reliable water in a buildable configuration. Infrastructure needs (septic, utilities) add startup cost but the land fundamentals are sound. At just over 6 acres there is minimal buffer for mistakes or expansion — this works best as a compact, intensive kitchen-garden homestead rather than a livestock or grain operation.

picks.headline: 18 acres with utilities and year-round water
picks.reason: 18 acres at $3,494/acre in Oregon County, MO is the lowest price per acre in this set that still includes confirmed utilities and year-round water — making it the clearest budget entry point for a functional homestead. Class 4 Alred-Gatewood soil at 12% slopes significantly limits row-crop agriculture and complicates construction planning, restricting this to pasture, hay, and livestock-based production. Remote Oregon County location means a long drive for medical care or major supply runs.

picks.headline: 21-acre parcel with utilities in Burton Branch watershed
picks.reason: Sand Hollow #18 offers the most acreage among the Bendavis-Poynor Class 4 cluster — 21.34 acres at $5,056/acre with electric and water available in the Burton Branch–Big Piney River watershed. Class 4 soil with severe agricultural limitations is the persistent weakness across this parcel group, making serious food production impractical and constraining the homestead to livestock, raised beds, and residential self-sufficiency. It is included as the strongest representative of a large group of similar parcels, but food-focused buyers should prioritize the higher-ranked options.
